findings.summary: Two parallel error systems (exceptions.py and errors.py) coexist without clear boundary, causing fragmented import patterns
evidence: infrastructure/core/exceptions.py provides a full exception class hierarchy (TemplateError, ValidationError, BuildError, etc.) with 48 importers
infrastructure/core/errors.py provides a separate InfraError dataclass system with string-formatted error constants
Only 4 files import from errors.py (pipeline.py, multi_project.py, cli.py, test_errors.py) while 48 import from exceptions.py
pipeline.py imports from both: 'from infrastructure.core.errors import ...' and uses exceptions from __init__.py
The two systems have overlapping semantic coverage (PIPELINE_STAGE_FAILED in errors.py vs PipelineError in exceptions.py) with no documented boundary rule
findings.suggestion: Establish an explicit boundary: errors.py for formatted log-message constants, exceptions.py for raiseable types. Document the rule in both module docstrings. Remove overlap where InfraError constants duplicate exception class intent.

findings.summary: pipeline_reporter.py performs a deferred local import of json inside a function body, inconsistent with module-level imports everywhere else
evidence: Line 677: 'import json' appears inside generate_test_report(), while json is already imported at module level (line 9)
All other infrastructure modules import json at module level; this is the only in-function import of a stdlib module
findings.suggestion: Remove the redundant in-function 'import json' on line 677; the module-level import already makes json available.

findings.summary: Large population of infrastructure modules missing get_logger call that 87/136 sibling modules use, creating a style island across multiple sub-packages
evidence: Blind packet sibling_behavior shows 87/136 infrastructure modules use get_logger; 49+ are flagged as outliers missing it
Modules with real logic (generator.py, pipeline_runner.py, package.py, platforms.py) lack loggers while their siblings all have them
reporting/__init__.py is flagged missing get_logger; other reporting siblings (pipeline_reporter.py, coverage_parser.py) all use it
findings.suggestion: Add 'from infrastructure.core.logging_utils import get_logger' and 'logger = get_logger(__name__)' to all logic-bearing modules that are missing it. Pure data/constants modules (models.py, config.py dataclass files) are acceptable exceptions.

findings.summary: Several scripts in scripts/ omit log_header/log_success calls that 14/18 and 12/18 sibling scripts use respectively
evidence: Blind packet scripts/ sibling_behavior: log_header count=14/18, log_success count=12/18
scripts/02_run_analysis.py missing get_logger, log_header, and log_success
scripts/execute_pipeline.py and generate_pipeline_reports.py missing log_header and log_success
scripts/show_project_info.py missing from __future__ import annotations and log_success
findings.suggestion: Align outlier scripts to the established pattern: import and call log_header at entry point and log_success at successful completion, matching the majority of sibling scripts.

findings.summary: Infrastructure modules inconsistently raise bare builtin exceptions (ValueError, RuntimeError) alongside the custom TemplateError hierarchy
evidence: infrastructure/reporting/manuscript_overview.py raises ValueError 5 times (e.g. 'PDF has no pages', 'No images provided') inside infrastructure layer
infrastructure/core/retry.py raises RuntimeError('failed after N attempts') instead of TemplateError subclass
infrastructure/core/menu.py raises ValueError('Empty choice'), ValueError('No valid choices found')
infrastructure/core/performance.py raises RuntimeError('Monitor not started')
The codebase has a well-defined custom exception hierarchy (18 raises of TemplateError subclasses) but these modules bypass it
findings.suggestion: Replace bare ValueError/RuntimeError raises inside infrastructure/ with appropriate TemplateError subclasses (e.g., ValidationError for invalid input, BuildError for pipeline failures). This makes callers' catch clauses predictable.

findings.summary: Multiple except-Exception blocks log a warning/error and return empty/None without propagating, silently hiding failures from callers
evidence: infrastructure/llm/cli/main.py lines 85-86 and 178-179: 'except Exception as e: logger.error(f"{e}")' with no re-raise or return of failure signal
infrastructure/publishing/cli.py lines 164-165 and 220-221: 'except Exception as e: logger.error(...)' with implicit None return
infrastructure/reporting/pipeline_reporter.py line 126-128: 'except Exception as e: logger.warning(...); summary["performance_analysis"] = {}' — swallows error silently with degraded empty result
Pattern count: 179 except-Exception occurrences across 58 infrastructure files, many with only log+return
findings.suggestion: At top-level CLI handlers, swallowing is acceptable. Inside library functions (pipeline_reporter, publishing internals), either re-raise as a typed TemplateError or return a typed result indicating failure so callers can react.

findings.summary: I/O boundary functions inconsistently return None on failure in some places and raise in others with no declared contract
evidence: _parse_coverage_json() returns None on JSONDecodeError/OSError (coverage_parser.py lines 262-266)
load_integrity_manifest() returns None on OSError/JSONDecodeError (integrity.py lines 710-712)
load_test_results() returns {} on OSError/JSONDecodeError (test_summary_generator.py lines 40-42)
Callers of these functions must check for None/empty separately; the three functions use three different empty sentinels (None, None, {}) for the same semantic condition
findings.suggestion: Establish a single convention for I/O helper return-on-failure: prefer Optional[T] with None for 'not found/unreadable' and document it explicitly. Consolidate the empty-dict vs None inconsistency.

findings.summary: Reporting and validation functions use Dict[str, Any] as both inputs and outputs extensively, creating implicit parameter bags with no type safety
evidence: Blind packet wide_param_bag_count=61 across the codebase

generate_pipeline_report() accepts test_results: Optional[Dict[str, Any]], validation_results: Optional[Dict[str, Any]], performance_metrics: Optional[Dict[str, Any]], error_summary: Optional[Dict[str, Any]], output_statistics: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] — 5 untyped dict bags in one signature

integrity.py functions return Dict[str, bool] and Dict[str, Any] where TypedDict variants already exist in the same file (BuildArtifactValidation, PermissionCheck, OutputCompleteness) but are not used for the primary functions
Dict[str, Any] appears 203 times across 49 infrastructure files
findings.suggestion: Promote the existing TypedDict classes (BuildArtifactValidation, PermissionCheck, OutputCompleteness) to be used as return types for the primary verify_* functions. For pipeline_reporter, extract a PipelineReportInput dataclass to replace the 5 optional dict parameters.

findings.summary: psutil is listed in the optional 'monitoring' dependency group but is guarded by try/except in two separate modules with duplicate fallback logic
evidence: Both health_check.py and performance.py independently do `try: import psutil except ImportError: psutil = None` with identical fallback assignment

pyproject.toml line 75-77 places psutil in optional 'monitoring' group, and line 98 includes it in default-groups — so it is always installed in normal use, making the try/except redundant in practice
The dual-guard pattern means neither module can share a single authoritative 'psutil available' check
findings.suggestion: Consolidate the psutil availability check into a single location (e.g., infrastructure/core/environment.py or a thin infrastructure/core/optional_deps.py) and import the result from there. Both health_check and performance would then share one authoritative boolean.

findings.summary: requests is a hard runtime dependency used both by the LLM client (HTTP calls to Ollama) and by health_check/_check_network, but the LLM subsystem is entirely optional
evidence: pyproject.toml lists `requests>=2.31.0` as a hard runtime dependency (line 43)
The entire llm/ subsystem is optional (requires Ollama), yet requests is not in the optional 'llm' group — it is pulled in for all consumers
health_check._check_network uses requests for HTTP availability testing, which is a lightweight use that could fall back to urllib
findings.suggestion: Move requests to the optional 'llm' dependency group and replace the health_check network probe with urllib.request, which is stdlib. This would avoid adding a non-stdlib dependency for users who never use the LLM integration.

findings.summary: scripts/01_run_tests.py (1520 LOC) and projects/code_project/scripts/optimization_analysis.py (1662 LOC) are flagged as zero-importer critical modules with no test coverage
evidence: existing_findings shows 'test_coverage: Untested critical module (1520 LOC, 0 importers)' for scripts/01_run_tests.py
existing_findings shows 'test_coverage: Untested critical module (1662 LOC, 0 importers)' for optimization_analysis.py

The testing section in holistic_context lists only one critical_untested file at the project level (cognitive_case_diagrams/src/visualization/styles.py), but the existing_findings reveal the two script-layer gaps are larger and higher-risk
findings.suggestion: Extract the core logic of scripts/01_run_tests.py into a testable module (e.g., infrastructure/reporting/test_runner.py) following the documented thin orchestrator pattern. Test that module directly. Similarly, move analysis logic in optimization_analysis.py into projects/code_project/src/.

findings.summary: tests/infra_tests/llm/test_logging.py contains MagicMock usage despite the project-wide no-mocks policy
evidence: Grep for MagicMock/mock.patch/unittest.mock found exactly one file: tests/infra_tests/llm/test_logging.py
CLAUDE.md states 'ABSOLUTE REQUIREMENT: Never use MagicMock, mocker.patch, unittest.mock, or any mocking framework'
All other test files (including test_sanitization.py and test_pipeline_reporter.py) successfully avoid mocks using real HTTP servers and real data
findings.suggestion: Replace the mock usage in test_logging.py with the real pytest-httpserver pattern already used in the LLM test suite (see conftest.py ollama_test_server fixture).

findings.summary: tests/infra_tests/reporting/ has no __init__.py while all peer test directories do, which can cause inconsistent test discovery
evidence: Glob for tests/infra_tests/**/__init__.py returns results for core/, publishing/, documentation/, llm/, project/, rendering/, validation/, scientific/, steganography/ — but NOT reporting/
tests/infra_tests/reporting/ contains 11 test files (3746 LOC) with no __init__.py
findings.suggestion: Add an empty tests/infra_tests/reporting/__init__.py to match all sibling test directories and prevent any import resolution edge cases during pytest collection.

findings.summary: Simple dataclass accessor methods carry multi-line docstrings with Args/Returns sections that restate the method signature
evidence: infrastructure/core/performance.py: ResourceUsage.to_dict() is a 6-line method mapping dataclass fields to a dict, yet it carries a docstring header 'Convert to dictionary.' — a restatement of the method name
infrastructure/core/health_check.py: SystemHealthChecker.get_health_status() has a full docstring with Args and Returns that repeats what the type signature already states (Dict[str, Any])
codebase avg comment ratio is 0.057 but the infrastructure/core/ subpackage has dense docstrings on every method regardless of complexity
findings.suggestion: Reserve docstrings for non-obvious behaviour, side effects, or domain rules. Remove or shrink docstrings on methods whose purpose and contract are completely expressed by their name and type signature.

findings.summary: Health-check helper methods emit structured log entries on entry and exit for operations that have no meaningful observability value at that granularity
evidence: holistic_context existing_findings flags infrastructure/llm/core/client.py for '6x Catch block that only logs (swallowed error)' — a classic AI-generated defensive logging pattern
logging_utils.py is imported by 115 modules; the sheer breadth suggests logging was added by default rather than selectively

infrastructure/core/__init__.py re-exports log_operation, log_stage, log_substep, log_success, log_header, log_stage_with_eta as a flat API surface — 8 logging entry points encourages over-instrumentation
findings.suggestion: Audit log call sites in health_check.py and llm/core/client.py for entry/exit logs that add no information beyond the function name. Remove those; retain only logs that carry meaningful state (e.g., actual values, error detail, timing).

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ has 26 files (8684 LOC) mixing pipeline orchestration, logging subsystem (4 files), health monitoring, security, credentials, menus, and progress — well beyond a coherent single-concern package
evidence: directory_profiles shows infrastructure/core/ at 26 files, total_loc 8684, avg_fan_in 9.7 — the highest fan-in of any package, creating high blast radius for any change
The logging subsystem is already split into 4 files (logging_utils.py, logging_formatters.py, logging_helpers.py, logging_progress.py) — an implicit sub-package that is not surfaced as one
infrastructure/core/__init__.py re-exports 35 symbols spanning 8 conceptual domains (checkpoint, config, environment, exceptions, health, logging, multi-project, performance, pipeline, progress)
Unrelated concerns — menu.py (interactive CLI menus), security.py (regex-based input scanning), credentials.py — all live alongside pipeline.py with no sub-grouping
findings.suggestion: Extract the logging quartet into infrastructure/core/logging/ as a sub-package. Consider moving menu.py to infrastructure/cli/ and security.py/credentials.py to infrastructure/security/. This would bring core/ down to a focused set of pipeline/lifecycle utilities.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/ contains two files named test_reporter.py and test_summary_generator.py that appear to be production modules despite their test_ prefix
evidence: directory_profiles for infrastructure/reporting/ lists zones as {'production': 12, 'test': 2} — the scanner itself classified these as test files inside a production directory
The files are test_reporter.py and test_summary_generator.py — naming pattern matches the test/ convention, yet they live under infrastructure/reporting/ and are imported by scripts/
coupling_matrix shows scripts/ imports from infrastructure/reporting/ 12 times, which may include these ambiguously named modules
findings.suggestion: Rename test_reporter.py to coverage_reporter.py and test_summary_generator.py to run_summary_generator.py (or similar) to avoid the test_ prefix collision. Update all import sites accordingly.

findings.summary: HealthChecker class placed inside sanitization.py rather than health_check.py, splitting ownership of system health across two unrelated modules
evidence: infrastructure/core/health_check.py defines SystemHealthChecker (the canonical health monitoring class)

infrastructure/llm/core/sanitization.py lines 197-305 define a second HealthChecker class with overlapping responsibilities (filesystem, dependencies, network, memory checks) inside a file whose stated purpose is 'Input sanitization and security utilities for LLM operations'
Both classes expose run_all_checks / get_health_status entry points over identical concern areas
findings.suggestion: Remove HealthChecker from sanitization.py and consolidate into infrastructure/core/health_check.py. Sanitization.py should only contain InputSanitizer and SecurityError.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/ contains 14 files with mixed, overlapping responsibilities including test reporting, pipeline reporting, validation reporting, error reporting, and executive reporting — boundaries are blurred
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py defines generate_test_report(), generate_validation_report(), generate_performance_report(), and generate_error_summary() — all logically belonging in dedicated sibling reporter files
test_reporter.py also defines generate_test_report() at line 213, creating a duplicate public API surface
14 files at 8461 LOC for a reporting layer is disproportionate; no clear ownership boundary between executive, pipeline, multi-project, and summary reporters
findings.suggestion: Each reporter file should own one report type end-to-end. Remove the redundant generate_test_report/generate_validation_report/etc. functions from pipeline_reporter.py; delegate to the dedicated sibling reporters.

findings.summary: StageResult dataclass uses None sentinel fields with __post_init__ fixup instead of dataclass field(default_factory=list) — leaks mutation risk and obscures intent at the seam
evidence: Lines 26-28: output_files, errors, warnings declared as None with type: ignore[assignment] comments
Lines 30-36: __post_init__ checks each for None and replaces with [] — standard Python dataclass mutable-default anti-pattern workaround
The type: ignore comments indicate the workaround is acknowledged but not fixed
findings.suggestion: Replace None sentinels with field(default_factory=list) for each of output_files, errors, warnings. Eliminates __post_init__ boilerplate and the type suppression.

findings.summary: generate_pipeline_report() accepts 9 positional/keyword parameters where 6 are optional dicts — should accept a structured context object at this boundary
evidence: Lines 273-283: function signature has stage_results, total_duration, repo_root, test_results, validation_results, performance_metrics, error_summary, output_statistics, project_name
Six of nine parameters are Optional[Dict[str, Any]] — structurally identical at the call site, distinguishable only by position/name
The holistic_context wide_param_bags confirms pipeline_reporter.py as a flag (wide_functions: 1)
findings.suggestion: Introduce a PipelineContext dataclass grouping the optional dict parameters. Callers pass one well-typed object instead of 6 keyword dicts.

findings.summary: generate_test_report() is defined in both pipeline_reporter.py and test_reporter.py, creating an ambiguous seam for callers
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py line 663: def generate_test_report(test_results: Dict[str, Any], output_dir: Path) -> Path
test_reporter.py line 213: def generate_test_report(...) — confirmed by grep
Both are in the reporting package's public surface; callers have no basis to choose between them
findings.suggestion: Remove generate_test_report from pipeline_reporter.py; pipeline_reporter should call test_reporter.generate_test_report() if needed.

findings.summary: generate_test_report() re-imports json at line 676 inside the function body, despite json already being imported at the top of the module
evidence: Line 9: import json (module-level)
Line 676: import json (inside generate_test_report body) — redundant and inconsistent
findings.suggestion: Remove the inline import; the module-level import is sufficient.

findings.summary: performance_context() context manager calls monitor.stop() in the finally block but discards the returned PerformanceMetrics — the metrics are unreachable after the context exits
evidence: Lines 153-166: yield monitor; finally: metrics = monitor.stop(); logger.debug(...) — metrics is a local variable that is never yielded or stored
Callers receive the monitor from yield but cannot access the final stop() metrics after the with-block
The docstring says 'callers can read PerformanceMetrics after exit' which is incorrect — monitor.stop() clears state (sets start_time = None)
findings.suggestion: Either yield (monitor, metrics_holder) as a mutable container, or yield the monitor and let the caller call monitor.stop() explicitly; or store metrics on the monitor object as monitor.last_metrics.

findings.summary: get_performance_warnings() iterates self.stages three times (slow, high-memory, high-cpu) where a single loop would suffice
evidence: Lines 271-307: three separate for stage in self.stages: loops, each looking at a different metric — slow_stage check, high_memory check, high_cpu check
Each loop is O(n); a single pass could collect all three warning types, reducing list traversals and improving locality
findings.suggestion: Merge into a single for loop that checks all three conditions per stage.

findings.summary: sanitization.py conflates LLM input sanitization (InputSanitizer, SecurityError) with general system health monitoring (HealthChecker), violating single-responsibility and making the module boundary confusing
evidence: Module docstring: 'Input sanitization and security utilities for LLM operations' — no mention of health checks
InputSanitizer (lines 20-188) is tightly LLM-specific; HealthChecker (lines 197-305) checks filesystem free space, dependency availability, network DNS, memory — entirely orthogonal concerns
Global singleton _health_checker created at module import time (line 310), triggering dependency checking on every LLM sanitization import
findings.suggestion: Move HealthChecker and related helpers (get_health_checker, run_health_check) to infrastructure/core/health_check.py. Keep sanitization.py focused on InputSanitizer and SecurityError.

findings.summary: performance.py provides two overlapping monitoring hierarchies: PerformanceMonitor/StagePerformanceTracker (psutil-based, stage-level) and CodeProfiler/ProfilingMetrics (tracemalloc/cProfile, function-level), each with a separate global instance pattern
evidence: PerformanceMonitor (line 76) and StagePerformanceTracker (line 195) provide stage-level monitoring; CodeProfiler (line 370) provides function-level profiling — three distinct classes solving overlapping problems
get_performance_monitor() (line 518) returns a global CodeProfiler; no equivalent global for PerformanceMonitor/StagePerformanceTracker

benchmark_function() at module level (line 575) creates a throwaway CodeProfiler() just to call its benchmark_function method — CodeProfiler.benchmark_function is effectively duplicated at module scope
ProfilingMetrics (function-level) and PerformanceMetrics (stage-level) are near-duplicate dataclasses with different field names for the same concepts
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into one monitoring class or clearly separate into two modules (stage_monitor.py, function_profiler.py) with non-overlapping responsibilities and no duplicated global accessors.

findings.summary: verify_output_completeness() hardcodes expected subdirectory names (pdf, figures, data, tex) rather than accepting them as parameters, making the function non-composable for varied project layouts
evidence: Lines 572-625: pdf_dir = output_dir / 'pdf'; figures_dir = output_dir / 'figures'; data_dir = output_dir / 'data'; tex_dir = output_dir / 'tex' — all hardcoded
The function checks html_files only in the output root (line 617) but all other checks use fixed subdirs — inconsistent internal structure
validate_build_artifacts() on line 480 correctly accepts expected_files as a parameter, showing the preferred pattern exists but was not applied here
findings.suggestion: Parameterize subdirectory names (pdf_subdir, figures_subdir, etc.) or accept an expected layout config object, matching the pattern used by validate_build_artifacts().
